title: Advanced RAG
description: Query rewriting, multi-hop retrieval, Graph RAG, agentic RAG, context compression, ACLs, and multi-tenant retrieval.

questions.title: When would you use query rewriting in RAG?
questions.scenario: Users ask follow-up questions like: what about the renewal clause?
questions.expectedAnswer: Query rewriting improves retrieval when user questions are vague, conversational, incomplete, or use different vocabulary than documents. The system can rewrite the query, expand acronyms, add domain terms, or generate multiple retrieval queries. It should be evaluated because bad rewrites can drift from user intent.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Advanced RAG?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise advanced rag setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Explain parent-child retrieval and its tradeoffs.
questions.scenario: A legal assistant retrieves exact clauses but needs surrounding contractual context to answer correctly.
questions.expectedAnswer: Parent-child retrieval indexes smaller child chunks for precise matching but returns a larger parent section for context. It balances retrieval precision with answer completeness. Tradeoffs include more complex indexing, duplicated context, citation granularity, and possible prompt bloat.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: What is multi-hop retrieval?
questions.scenario: A user asks which vendors affected by a policy also have contracts expiring this quarter.
questions.expectedAnswer: Multi-hop retrieval decomposes questions requiring evidence from multiple sources or steps. The system retrieves initial evidence, uses it to form follow-up queries, and combines evidence to answer. It is useful for complex enterprise questions but adds latency, orchestration complexity, and failure points.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: When is Graph RAG useful?
questions.scenario: An enterprise needs to answer questions about dependencies between applications, owners, vendors, and risks.
questions.expectedAnswer: Graph RAG is useful when relationships between entities matter, such as people, systems, policies, vendors, and dependencies. It can improve multi-hop reasoning and explainability by retrieving neighborhoods or paths. It adds graph construction, entity resolution, freshness, and governance complexity.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: How do you design ACL-aware RAG?
questions.scenario: A bank wants employees to search documents while respecting department and deal-room permissions.
questions.expectedAnswer: ACL-aware RAG enforces permissions during retrieval, not after generation. Chunks carry tenant, source, document, and permission metadata. Retrieval filters by user identity, groups, and policy before reranking and prompt assembly. The system must handle permission changes, deletions, audit logs, and tests for leakage.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: How would you explain context compression in a Advanced RAG interview?
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through context compression for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define context compression, explain why it matters for advanced rag, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations.
